questions.q: Which three properties does TLS provide?
How many round trips does a full TLS 1.3 handshake require?
Which certificate in a chain is self-signed?
Why was RSA key exchange removed in TLS 1.3?
questions.a: It did not provide forward secrecy and was less secure than ephemeral key exchange.

questions.q: What is the main risk of 0-RTT resumption?
What does the certificate chain establish?
questions.a: A trust path from the leaf certificate back to a trusted root

questions.q: Why is SNI often sent in plaintext?
questions.a: The server must know which certificate to present before encryption is established.

questions.q: What role does RSA play in ECDHE_RSA cipher suites?
questions.a: It signs the handshake parameters, not the key exchange itself.

questions.q: What does mTLS add on top of standard TLS?
Why does TLS 1.3 simplify the handshake compared with TLS 1.2?
questions.a: It removes legacy negotiation paths and moves to a faster, safer default flow.

questions.q: Why is PFS valuable if a long-term key is compromised later?
questions.a: Past sessions remain protected because their traffic keys were derived from ephemeral secrets.

questions.q: Why is revocation harder than certificate issuance?
questions.a: Clients and intermediates may cache certificates, so revocation data must be checked and distributed reliably.

questions.q: What tradeoff does 0-RTT make for speed?
questions.a: It lowers latency but allows early data to be replayed by an attacker.

questions.q: Why do public CAs and certificate transparency logs both matter?
questions.a: CAs issue certificates, while CT logs make issuance auditable and easier to monitor.

questions.q: Why can mTLS be a good fit for internal service-to-service traffic?
questions.a: It gives strong machine identity without relying only on bearer tokens.
